title: High Rise Shelf
summary: A small wall shelf where the shelves float off a back board on spacer blocks — a West Elm-style display piece from two short boards and an afternoon.
description:
This is a quick, high-impact wall shelf: a 1x4 back board with short spacer blocks that hold a row of little 1x8 shelves proud of the wall, so they appear to float in a stepped, gallery-ish arrangement. It knocks off a pricey retailer look for the cost of two six-foot boards and a handful of screws.

The only technique worth naming is hiding your fasteners. Screw the spacers to the back board from behind, through the back, so no screw heads show on the front — predrill so the short blocks don't split. If you'd rather, a brad nailer works here too. The shelves themselves can be centered on their spacers or offset for a more dynamic look; there's no wrong answer, so lay them out before committing.

Hanging is the one place to be careful because a shelf carries whatever you set on it. Screw through the front of the shelf into a wall stud where you can; where there's no stud, use a proper drywall anchor rated for the load. Done right it's genuinely sturdy despite looking delicate — a nice display piece for small plants, books, or objects.

tools.note: Predrill the short spacer blocks so they don't split.
So the shelf hangs true on the wall.

materials.name: 1x8 board, 6 ft
materials.note: Shelves. A 1x6 or 1x12 works too — adjust shelf length.

materials.name: Drywall anchors
materials.note: Where you can't hit a stud.

cutList.note: A 1x6 or 1x12 lets you go shallower or deeper.

steps.title: Cut the back board, spacers, and shelves
steps.body: From the 6 ft 1x4, cut one back board 36 3/4" long, two end spacers 3 1/2" long, and four shelf spacers 6 1/2" long. That is 69 3/4" of a 72" board, so the whole back-and-spacer set comes out of the one piece with a little to spare. From the 6 ft 1x8, cut five shelves 8 1/2" long; a 1x8 is 7 1/4" wide, so each finished shelf is 8 1/2" across the wall by 7 1/4" deep. Square every crosscut with the speed square, because the spacers are short blocks and an out-of-square block tips its shelf visibly. Label the two 3 1/2" end spacers so they do not get mixed in with the four 6 1/2" ones.

steps.title: Attach the spacers
steps.body: Lay the 36 3/4" back board flat and mark where each of the six spacers goes, remembering they have to carry five shelves in a stepped arrangement. Dry-lay the shelves on those marks and look at the whole composition before you commit, since the source leaves the spacing and the stagger to you. Glue the less-attractive face of each spacer to the back board and drive 1-1/4" screws from behind, through the back board into the block, so no heads show on the front. Predrill every one of those holes: a 3 1/2" or 6 1/2" offcut of 1x4 splits readily when a screw lands near its end. A brad nailer works here too if you would rather not fill holes.

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: Set each 8 1/2" shelf on its spacer with the 7 1/4" dimension running out from the wall, centred on the block or offset to one side. Lay all five out and eyeball the whole arrangement before fastening, because those offsets are what make the piece read as a stepped display rather than a plain rack. Fasten each shelf with 1-1/4" screws and glue, predrilling near the block ends again. Check each shelf sits level across its depth before the glue grabs; a shelf that tips forward will slide whatever you set on it.

steps.title: Finish and hang
steps.body: Fill the screw holes, sand everything smooth, and finish the shelf before it goes on the wall, which is far easier than working around it afterwards. To hang it, hold it up, mark a level line, and drive 2" screws through the front of a shelf into a wall stud wherever a stud lands; where there is no stud, use a drywall anchor rated for the load you actually intend to put on it. Check it with the level before the last screw goes home. Hung this way it carries far more than its delicate look suggests, but it is only ever as strong as what it is anchored to.

images.alt: Small wall shelf with shelves floating off a back board on spacers
